coredata.dc:title: Gadolinium doped carbon dots for anti-gram-negative bacteria and visible light photodynamic enhancement of antibacterial effect
coredata.prism:publicationName: Spectrochimica Acta Part A: Molecular and Biomolecular Spectroscopy
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2024 Elsevier B.V. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.prism:publisher: Elsevier B.V. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.dc:creator: Miao, Yanming
Zhang, Tao
Zhao, Xujuan
Sun, Xiaojie
Lv, Jinzhi
coredata.dc:description: Infection with gram-negative bacteria is the main source of the most serious infectious pathogens. Developing new antibacterial materials that break through their external membranes and stay in the bacterial body to result in an antibacterial effect is the key to achieving high efficiency against Gram-negative bacteria. A Gd-doped carbon dot (GRCD) was prepared using the approved therapeutic diagnostic agents Rose Bengal (RB) and gadolinium ions (Gd3+), which was used to resist Gram-negative bacteria (e.g. E. coli, Escherichia coli). GRCD not only showed strong antibacterial activity by destroying the external membranes of E. coli (inhibition rate against E. coli was 92.0 % at 20 μg/mL) but also bound to E. coli DNA and generated single oxygen (1O2) (quantum yield was 0.50) through visible light-driven catalysis, thus decomposing the DNA of E. coli and further enhancing the antibacterial performance of GRCD. Under visible light conditions, the inhibition rate against E. coli reached 95.8 % at a low concentration of 2.5 μg/mL, without obvious cytotoxicity to NIH3T3 cells. The use of GRCD in treating wound infections in mice caused by E. coli was quite good, without side reactions on the mice’s essential organs. In this study, a new approach has been provided to the design and synthesis of carbon dot nanocomposites for use against Gram-negative bacteria.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Gram-negative bacteria
Gd-doped carbon dots
Visible-light-driven photodynamic bacterial resistance

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Effect of GRCD on the cell membrane
Inhibition of GRCD to E. Coli
Interaction between GRCD and E. Coli
Inhibition mechanism of GRCD on E. Coli
Practical applications of GRCD with antibacterial properties

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Characterization of GRCD (A) TEM image of GRCD (Inset: distribution of particle size); (B) XRD pattern of GRCD; (C) FT-IR spectra of GRCD and RB; (D) XPS full spectrum of GRCD; (E) C 1s, (F) O 1s, (G) Gd high-resolution XPS spectra; (H) ZeTa potential maps of GRCD and RB; (I) Different element proportions of GRCD; (J) UV–visible absorption spectrum (green line), optimal fluorescence excitation spectrum (blue line) and emission spectrum (orange line) of GRCD in aqueous solution (inset: photo of GRCD aqueous solution under natural light); (K) Fluorescence three-dimensional contour map of GRCD aqueous solution; (L) Fluorescence decay curve of GRCD.

The inhibitory performance of GRCD on E. coli. (A) Inhibitory effect and (B) Inhibition rates of GRCD, RB, and RCD on E. coli in the visible light; (C) Inhibitory effect and (D) Inhibition rates of GRCD, RB, and RCD on E. coli under dark conditions; (E) Control of the effects of light and dark conditions on the growth of E. coli; SEM images of (F) the control group and (G) GRCD-treated E. coli; (H) Inhibition of S. aureus by GRCD under light or dark conditions; (I) Inhibition rate of GRCD on S. aureus under light or dark conditions. ns Indicating p > 0.05; *Indicating p < 0.05; **Indicating p < 0.01; ***Indicating p < 0.001.

(A) Diagram of Gram-negative bacterial cell membranes; (B) LC-MS of PC after GRCD treatment; (C) Laser confocal images of E. coli under PBS and GRCD conditions (yellow: luminescence of carbon dots); (D) Effect of Gd3+ on E. coli and S. aureus in dark and light situations; (E) Inhibition rates of Gd3+ on E. coli and S. aureus in dark or light situations (12.7 μM is equivalent to the Gd3+ content in 30 μg/mL GRCD); (F) TEM images of slices of E. coli under PBS, GRCD conditions; (G) Absorption spectra of DNA at different concentrations of GRCD; (H) Absorption spectra of DNA combined with EB at different GRCD concentrations. ns Indicating p > 0.05.

(A) Effect of GRCD on the gray value of DNA gel electrophoresis under dark and light conditions; (B) Absorbance of TMB under different GRCD treatment time; (C) EPR spectrum of singlet oxygen; (D) Phosphorescence three-dimensional spectrum of GRCD; (E) Phosphorescence lifetime of GRCD; (F) Mechanism diagram of GRCD generating singlet oxygen;(G-L) Calculation of 1O2 quantum yield of GRCD in water: (G) UV–visible absorption spectrum of GRCD+DPBF under visible light irradiation at different time; (H) Decomposition rate of DPBF by GRCD; (I) Absorption spectrum of GRCD; (J) UV–visible absorption spectrum of PB under visible light irradiation at different time; (K) Decomposition rate curve of DPBF by PB; (L) Absorption spectrum of PB.

Photodynamic wound-healing properties of GRCD. (A) Schematic diagram of wound process in GRCD photodynamic therapy; (B) Wound photos and (C) healing rates of PBS control group, PBS+Visible light group, GRCD+Dark group, and GRCD+Visible light group on day 0, 3, 7, and 14. (D) Morphological evaluation of wound regeneration tissues in PBS control group, PBS+Visible light group, GRCD+Dark group, and GRCD+Visible light group on the 14th day (Scale bar: 100 μm). ns Indicating p > 0.05; *Indicating p < 0.05; **Indicating p < 0.01; ***Indicating p < 0.001.
Diagram of the principle and application of GRCD against Gram-negative bacteria.

head.ce:title: Gadolinium doped carbon dots for anti-gram-negative bacteria and visible light photodynamic enhancement of antibacterial effect

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Life Science, Shanxi Normal University, Taiyuan 030006, PR China
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of Life Science, Shanxi Normal University, Taiyuan 030006, PR China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: In this study, a Gd-doped carbon dot (GRCD) was used to resist Gram-negative bacteria. GRCD not only showed strong antibacterial activity by destroying the outer membranes of E. coli, but also bound to E. coli DNA and generated single oxygen (1O2) through visible light-driven catalysis, thus decomposing the DNA of E. coli and further enhancing the antibacterial performance of GRCD.

Infection with gram-negative bacteria is the main source of the most serious infectious pathogens. Developing new antibacterial materials that break through their external membranes and stay in the bacterial body to result in an antibacterial effect is the key to achieving high efficiency against Gram-negative bacteria. A Gd-doped carbon dot (GRCD) was prepared using the approved therapeutic diagnostic agents Rose Bengal (RB) and gadolinium ions (Gd3+), which was used to resist Gram-negative bacteria (e.g. E. coli, Escherichia coli). GRCD not only showed strong antibacterial activity by destroying the external membranes of E. coli (inhibition rate against E. coli was 92.0 % at 20 μg/mL) but also bound to E. coli DNA and generated single oxygen (1O2) (quantum yield was 0.50) through visible light-driven catalysis, thus decomposing the DNA of E. coli and further enhancing the antibacterial performance of GRCD. Under visible light conditions, the inhibition rate against E. coli reached 95.8 % at a low concentration of 2.5 μg/mL, without obvious cytotoxicity to NIH3T3 cells. The use of GRCD in treating wound infections in mice caused by E. coli was quite good, without side reactions on the mice’s essential organs. In this study, a new approach has been provided to the design and synthesis of carbon dot nanocomposites for use against Gram-negative bacteria.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: With the fast growth of multidrug-resistant (MDR) bacteria, as well as the continuous decrease in the discovery of new antibiotics, the human ability to control bacterial resistance is weakening. Gram-negative bacteria occupy the largest proportion of pathogens that pose the greatest threat to untreatable MDR infections (four of the six ESKAPE pathogens) [1], and nine of the twelve priority pathogens specified by the World Health Organization are Gram-negative bacteria. However, Gram-negative bacteria are inherently advantageous in resisting the effects of antibiotics, as their cell walls have at least two unique features that help them more effectively repel small-molecule antibiotics than the walls of Gram-positive cells [2]. First, the external membranes of Gram-negative bacteria are composed of asymmetric bilayer lipid membranes tightly wrapped by lipopolysaccharide (LPS), which can be used as a strong barrier to prevent antibiotic compounds from entering Gram-negative bacteria [3,4]. Second, even if antibiotic compounds enter the external membranes, drug efflux pumps are able to get rid of antibiotics from the periplasm and cytoplasm to avoid their retention. Moreover, the external membranes and efflux pumps work together to make Gram-negative bacteria impermeable to various antibiotics [3,5,6]. It is difficult to find new antibiotics against Gram-negative bacteria. The FDA has not approved a new antibiotic to treat fatal Gram-negative bacteria infections since 1968. As a consequence, new antibacterial materials that can break through the external membranes of Gram-negative bacteria and stay inside the bacteria with an antibacterial effect need to be developed. This is also the key to improving the efficiency against Gram-negative bacteria. Currently, antibacterial materials based on nanomaterials have become a research hotspot [7–12], which lays the foundation for developing new materials against Gram-negative bacteria.

Especially, carbon dot (CD) nanomaterials have high biocompatibility and water solubility, as well as fluorescence stability. In particular, on the surface of CDs, there are many functional groups of carboxyl, hydroxyl, and epoxy that are able to obtain multifunctional CD nanomaterials through modification [13–17], so it is an ideal choice and research hotspot to develop antibacterial materials [18–20]. However, single CDs often do not have an antibacterial function, and the antibacterial performance is not ideal. The rare earth elements and their compounds represented by lanthanum (La) are characterized by low toxicity and dose antibiotics, as well as broad-spectrum [21–25]. Producing multifunctional nanomaterials by doping rare earth elements into nanomaterials has attracted widespread concern from researchers [23,26–31]. Gd has been widely used as a contrast agent for magnetic resonance imaging and also used in radiotherapy sensitization, owing to its distinct chemical and physical properties [32–35]. Moreover, it is an approved element with relatively good biocompatibility for clinical use, butalso easy to coordinate with O, and N or be doped into nanoparticles to prepare specifically functionalized nanomaterials. Therefore, Gd can be combined with CD to form nanocomposites, and new materials against Gram-negative bacteria can be developed.

In this study, a polymeric carbon dot (GRCD) was synthesized using the approved therapeutic diagnostic agents Rose Bengal (RB) and Gd3+ as raw materials, and used for growth inhibition of Gram-negative bacteria (E. coli). Based on the comparison between free RB molecules, and the carbon dots synthesized by RB alone (RCDs), the GRCD showed higher dark-state antibacterial activity and visible light photodynamic antibacterial activity. Specifically, Gd3+ bound to LPS in the external membrane of E. coli and destroyed the structure of the external membrane, thus playing a key role in allowing GRCD to break through the cell membrane barrier and enter the body of E. coli. In addition, GRCD intercalated with DNA after entering E. coli, and generated singlet oxygen (1O2) through photosensitive catalytic oxidation under visible light irradiation, further decomposing DNA and ultimately inhibiting the growth of E. coli (Scheme 1 ).

Gadolinium nitrate (Gd(NO3)3·6H2O) and 3,3′,5,5′-Tetramethylbenzidine (TMB) were bought from Shanghai Macklin Biochemical Technology Co., Ltd. (China). Rose bengal (RB) was bought from Sangon Biotech (Shanghai) Co., Ltd. L-Alpha- Hosphatidylcholine (PC) was supplied by Yuanye Biotech Co., Ltd. (Shanghai, China). MonTrackTM D2000 Plus DNA Ladder was offered by Monad Biotech Co., Ltd. (Suzhou, China). Escherichia coli strain (E. coli, ATCC25922) and Staphylococcus aureus strain (S. aureus, CMCC(B)26003) were bought from Luwei Technology Co., Ltd. (Shanghai, China). Female C57BL/6JNifdc mice were bought from Sipeifu Biotech Co., Ltd. (Beijing, China), while the ultrapure water was produced from a UPT-I-20 T water machine.

A Thermo Escalab 250XI X-ray photoelectron spectrometer (XPS, Thermo Fisher Scientific, USA), an Ultima IV-185 X-ray powder diffractometer (XRD, Rigaku Co., Ltd., Japan), a Nicolet IS10 Fourier transform infrared spectrometer (FT-IR, Nicolet, USA), a JEM1200EX transmission electron microscope (TEM, JEOL Company, Japan), and a Zetasizer Nano ZS90 zeta potential analyzer (Malvern Company, UK) were used here. The surface morphology of E. coli was measured with a Hitachi SU8020 scanning electron microscope (SEM, Hitachi, Japan). UV–visible spectra were recorded on a UV-1800PC UV–visible spectrophotometer (Mapada, China), and the spectra of fluorescence and phosphorescence, as well as phosphorescence lifetime were measured with a Cary Eclipse fluorescence spectrophotometer (Agilent, USA). LC-MS was performed using an Agilent 1290II-6460 high-performance liquid chromatograph (Agilent, USA). The fluorescence microscopy images of E. coli were photographed on a Zeiss LSM-880 laser confocal microscope (Zeiss, Germany). Samples were dried on a ZX-LGJ-1A freeze dryer (Shanghai Zhixin Experimental Instrument Technology Co., Ltd., China). EPR spectra were measured on an A300-10/12 electron paramagnetic resonance instrument (Bruker, Germany).

15 mL of ethanol was used to dissolve Gd(NO3)3·6H2O (100 mg) and RB (220 mg) Next, the solution was put into a Teflon-lined high-pressure tank and then heated in an oven at 180 °C for 12 h. After it was cooled to room temperature naturally, 50 mL of ultrapure water was added and thoroughly stirred for 10 min. After that, the solution was centrifuged at a high speed. Finally, the supernatant was purified by a dialysis membrane (MWCO 500–1000) and then dried under a vacuum.

E. coli and S. aureus as experimental strains in Luria-Bertani (LB) liquid media were put overnight in a constant temperature shaking incubator at 37 °C first. Afterward, the E. coli and S. aureus suspensions in the logarithmic growth stage were (4 × 108 CFU/mL) diluted to 4 × 104 CFU mL−1 with sterile water for subsequent use. The plate counting approach was adopted to assess the antibacterial activity of GRCD. GRCD at different concentrations (0, 2.5, 5, 10, 20 μg/mL) was thoroughly mixed with bacterial suspensions (2 × 108 CFU mL−1, 500 μL). Then the mixed solutions were irradiated under visible light for 5 min (50 mW/cm2) after being incubated at 37 °C for 5 min. Finally, the treated bacterial suspensions (100 µL) were evenly spread on LB agar plates and then incubated at 37 °C for 12 h. Then the bacteria were photographed and counted. Each experiment was repeatedly done three times. Consequently, the bacteria survival rate was computed as follows:
B a c t e r i a l v i a b i l i t y
%
=
T r e a t m e n t g r o u p b a c t e r i a l c o u n t
C o n t r o l g r o u p b a c t e r i a l c o u n t
× 100 %

After fully mixing the standard MonTrackTM D2000 Plus DNA Ladder with an appropriate amount of GRCD, we set up a blank group, a blank + Visible light group, a GRCD+Dark group, and a GRCD+Visible light group. After a reaction for 5 min, the samples were put into the electrophoresis tank, and then photographed, followed by the calculation of the gray value.

An E. coli group and an E. coli + GRCD group were set. After acting with GRCD for 5 min, the samples were centrifuged at a rotational speed of 3000 r/min. Then we removed the supernatant and used 1 mL of PBS solution to wash the resulting samples three times. Next, the samples were washed three times with deionized water and finally added with 1 mL of 25 % glutaraldehyde fixative. After storage at 4 °C for 12 h, we observed the samples and photographed them with a laser confocal microscope. At the same time, E. coli was dried and observed under SEM.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Effect of GRCD on the cell membrane
ce:section.ce:para: PC (2 mg) was suspended in 2 mL of physiological saline. Next, 1 mL of the PC solution was taken out to mix with 2 mL of a 250 μg/mL GRCD solution. Then the mixture was placed in a shaker at 240 r/min and 37 °C for 4 h and incubated on a shaking table. After centrifugation at 12,000 r/min for 5 min, the samples were completely suspended in 1 mL of chloroform, and shaken thoroughly for 5 min. Then the PC molecules were extracted, and the suspensions were centrifuged at a rotational speed of 6000 r/min for 5 min. Finally, we obtained the supernatant for LC-MS [4,6].

To exploring the mechanism of light-responsive oxidase-like performance of GRCD, we carried out experiments on reactive oxygen species (ROS) capture. Different capture agents were added to the GRCD/TMB system under optimal conditions, containing 5 mM of mannitol (•OH scavenger), 2.5 mg/mL of tryptophan (1O2 scavenger), and 200 U/mL of hydrogen peroxidase (H2O2 scavenger) and 300 U/mL of superoxide dismutase (SOD, O2 •– scavenger). In the end, we placed the mixed solution under visible light for irradiation for different periods of time and recorded the absorbance at 652 nm.

DPBF was utilized to measure the quantum yield of 1O2, and phloxine (PB) was adopted as the standard photosensitizer for calculations. Then, PB or GRCD at a suitable concentration was added into a DPBF (60 μM) solution, and PB or GRCD at a proper concentration was added to make sure that enough absorbance was available at 400–700 nm in the mixtures. Then the mixed solutions were irradiated under visible light. After that, the UV–vis spectra at different periods of time were recorded. The relevant 1O2 oxygen quantum yield of GRCD was calculated as follows[13,36].
Φ =
Φ
PB
K
A
PB
/
K
PB
A

Where K and KPB refer to the constants of decomposition rate constants of DPBF by GRCD and PB respectively; while A and APB indicate the absorption spectral areas of GRCD and PB respectively (depending on integrating the light absorption bands in 400–700 nm); ΦPB is the 1O2 quantum yield of PB (0.65 in water).

Mouse fibroblast NIH3T3 cells were seeded in a 96-well cell plate at 5000 cells/well. After the cell adhesion was completed, various concentrations of GRCD (0, 2.5, 5, 10, 20, 30 ug/mL) were used for fluid exchange treatment for 24 h, and cultured in a 37 °C, 5 % CO2 incubator. Then a medium containing a 10 % CCK-8 reaction solution was used to replace the culture medium. The culture plates were incubated in the incubator for 4 to 6 h. The absorbance at 450 nm was measured by a microplate reader, and the calculation of cell viability is given below:
C e l l v i a b i l i t y
%
= O
D
sample
/ O
D
control
× 100

Female C57BL/6JNifdc mice aged 6 to 8 weeks were classified into 4 groups: PBS group, PBS+Visible light group, GRCD+Dark group, and GRCD+Visible light group. Sterile surgical scissors were used to cut out circle-shaped skin with a diameter of 1.0 cm, and the wound was deep to the fascia (anesthesia respiration). Then 50 μL of E. coli (107 CFU/mL) was injected to cover the wound. After the treatment for 3, 7 or 14 days, the wound healing rate of each group was computed as below: (initial wound area − wound area after healing)/initial wound area × 100 %. Then the mouse wound tissues on the 14th day were harvested and cultured in 5 mL of sterile physiological saline. Another part of the tissues was used for hematoxylin-eosin (HE) staining and immunofluorescence analysis. In HE staining, mouse wound tissues were soaked in 4 % paraformaldehyde, then routinely dehydrated, and embedded in paraffin. After that, continuous sections with a thickness of 3 μm were made, stained with hematoxylin-eosin, and observed under microscopy. Besides, we also analyzed mouse liver and kidney function indicators: alanine aminotransferase (ALT), albumin (ALB), urea (UREA), total bilirubin (TBIL), creatinine (CREA), and aspartate aminotransferase (AST).

0.25 g of polyvinyl alcohol (PVA) was specifically dissolved into 2.5 mL of ultrapure water, and mixed with 50 μL of a GRCD solution (5 mg/mL). A GRCD/PVA solution was made under uniform stirring. In the end, the GRCD/PVA solution was coated on the substrate of glass, and after hours of drying, a GRCD/PVA composite film was obtained, which was used for the measurement of GRCD phosphorescence spectrum and phosphorescence lifetime.

TEM shows that the average particle size of GRCD is approximately 2.07 ± 0.79 nm, and there are no evident lattice fringes (Fig. 1 A), suggesting that CNDs are possibly polymer carbon dots. Moreover, XRD reveals that CNDs’s peak appeared at 23 ° (Fig. 1B), which is regarded as a partial order in the carbon dots from the covalent crosslinking skeleton generated from polymerization [37]. According to the results, it can be seen that CNDs are polymer carbon dots, and the TEM results are approved.

The FTIR spectra of RB and GRCD (Fig. 1C) indicate the changes in the composition and functional groups of GRCD. GRCD shows characteristic peaks of CC (1546, 1552, 1342 cm−1) in benzene, a new peak of carbonyl CO at 1614 cm−1, as well as a new peak of CC at 759 and 881 cm−1. To further illustrate the structure of GRCD, we further analyzed the XPS spectrum of GRCD (Fig. 1D–G). C, Cl, I, O, and Gd still exist after the formation of GRCD, and I 3d and Cl 2p still have strong signals, manifesting that the solvothermal treatment does not thoroughly damage the structure of molecular based on RB fluorescein (Fig. 1D). The high-resolution energy spectrum of C1s (Fig. 1E) has 3 characteristic peaks at 284.8, 286.4, and 288.8 eV, which correspond to CC/CC, CO, and COOH respectively. The high-resolution energy spectrum of O1s (Fig. 1F) can be decomposed into peaks at 532.0 and 533.7 eV, respectively corresponding to COH and CO. The characteristic peak of Gd 4d appeared in GRCD (Fig. 1G) proving the existence of Gd3+. The above FT-IR and XPS results show that the oxygen in the carboxyl group and phenolic hydroxyl group of RB coordinates with Gd3+, changing the surface potential from − 28.87 mV in RB to − 1.15 mV in GRCD (Fig. 1H), where the Gd3+ with an atom proportion of 0.63 % (Figs. 1I and S1) may play a decisive role in the potential change.

The spectra of UV–vis absorption and photoluminescence, and the curves of fluorescent lifetime attenuation of GRCD in aqueous solutions were studied to analyze the fluorescent characteristics of GRCD in aqueous solutions. The peak of UV absorption of a GRCD aqueous solution and optimal excitation and emission is located at 520 nm and 473 nm, respectively, and the optimal emission peak is located at 551 nm (Fig. 1J and K), exhibiting yellow-green fluorescence under visible light (inset in Fig. 1J). Since there is a large overlap in the absorption spectra, excitation spectra, and emission spectra of GRCD and RB (Figs. S2 and 1J), it further implies that no molecular structure has been destroyed by the solvothermal treatment based on RB fluorescein. Since GRCD does not have an obvious crystal structure, most of its fluorescence emission comes from molecular and surface states. Under the wavelengths of optimal excitation and emission, the average fluorescence lifetime (double exponential function fitting) is 4.16 ns (Fig. 1L).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Inhibition of GRCD to E. Coli
ce:section.ce:para: To better compare and study the inhibitory performances of GRCD on E. coli, we also studied the inhibitory performances of RB and synthesized the carbon dots from RB (RCD) on E. coli. The inhibition rate of GRCD against E. coli under the irradiation of LED visible light reached 95.8 % at a low concentration of 2.5 μg/mL, while the inhibition rates of RB and RCD against E. coli were only 24.7 % and 35.6 %, respectively (Fig. 2 A and B), indicating we synthesized GRCD with stronger resistance against E. coli. However, under no light conditions, the inhibition rate of GRCD on E. coli was only 92.0 % at 20 μg/mL (Fig. 2C and 2D). With the absence of GRCD, visible light had no obvious effect on the growth of E. coli (Fig. 2E), suggesting that visible light helps improve the anti-E. coli performance of GRCD and that GRCD has certain photodynamic resistance against E. coli. The above results indicate not only GRCD has a good anti-E. coli effect, but also visible light can significantly enhance the performance of GRCD in inhibiting E. coli (Fig. 2B and D). However, the E. coli resistance of GRCD mainly comes from the structural properties of GRCD (the antibacterial rate in the dark was 66.7 % at 2.5 μg/mL), and the visible light photodynamic E. coli resistance was promoted (antibacterial contribution rate was 29.1 % at 2.5 μg/mL). To better illustrate the special interaction between GRCD and Gram-negative bacteria (E. coli), we also studied the antibacterial effect of GRCD on the typical Gram-positive S. aureus (Fig. 2H and I). Results show that GRCD has a weak antibacterial effect on S. aureus. Only under visible light irradiation can GRCD have an obvious antibacterial effect on S. aureus. This result indicates the interaction of GRCD with E. coli at the structural level is more severe than that with S. aureus. Furthermore, the antibacterial function of GRCD on S. aureus mainly came from Photodynamic antibacterial. In addition, from the SEM images before and after GRCD acted on E. coli (Fig. 2F and G), the cell membranes of E. coli were ruptured in the presence of GRCD, which may be one of the important reasons for the death of E. coli.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Interaction between GRCD and E. Coli
ce:section.ce:para: The external membranes of Gram-negative bacteria are composed of asymmetric bilayer lipid membranes tightly wrapped by lipopolysaccharide (LPS) (Fig. 3 A), and the LPS carbon chains outside the external membranes can form a complex polar network [3,6]. Consequently, the Gram-negative bacteria can resist the entry of external hydrophobic components, forming an effective protective barrier that can prevent antibacterial drugs from harming the bacteria. Breaking through the LPS network layer of the external membranes of the bacteria is one of the key steps for further antibacterial action [4,6]. To better study the interaction mechanism between GRCD and LPS, we used phosphatidylcholine (PC) liposomes to simulate LPS’s external layer of Gram-negative bacteria and analyzed it with LC-MS after the incubation with GRCD. In addition to the peak of PC molecules, the dephosphorylation peak of PC was also detected (Figs. S3 and 3B), indicating that GRCD can dephosphorylate PC, creating favorable conditions for GRCD to enter the bacteria.

Fig. 3C shows a laser confocal microscope photo of E. coli after adding RCD or GRCD. There was no luminescence of RCD in E. coli after RCD was added. However, after the addition of GRCD, the luminescence of GRCD appeared in E. coli, indicating that GRCD entered the body of E. coli. However, RCD didn’t enter the body of E. coli, which revealed that the doping of Gd3+ may be the reason why GRCD can enter the body of E. coli. The key factor may be that the combination of Gd3+ in GRCD and LPS breaks through the external membrane barrier of E. coli. The culture results of Gd3+ alone with E. coli and Gram-positive S. aureus (Fig. 3D and E) show an obvious antibacterial effect of Gd3+ on E. coli, but basically no antibacterial effect on S. aureus (Gram-positive bacteria have no LPS layer). This result further implies that Gd3+ is the key factor for GRCD to break through the LPS barrier of E. coli.

Furthermore, the TEM images before and after adding GRCD to E. coli (Fig. 3F) reveal that GRCD after entering E. coli was mainly concentrated in the nuclear region and bound to the DNA, indicating the interaction of GRCD with the DNA of E. coli. After adding GRCD to the DNA solution, the absorbance increased with the rise of GRCD concentration, the color enhancement effect appeared, and the maximum absorption blue-shifted from 260 to 257 nm (Fig. 3G). The coloring effect is a unique spectral property of DNA closely related to its double helix structure. Therefore, GRCD affects the double helix structure of DNA. The reason may be that the Gd3+ in GRCD combines with the phosphate groups of the DNA backbone and the overall GRCD has a weak negative charge, which stretches the DNA and produces a color enhancement effect. The blue shift of the maximum absorption peak indicates that GRCD binds to DNA in an insertion manner. The reason may be that the insertion of GRCD into the DNA double helix bases results in changes in DNA electrons. Ethidium bromide (EB) is a classic DNA insertion fluorescent probe. To further determine the interaction mode between GRCD and DNA, we introduced the EB probe for competition experiments. When the action modes of GRCD and EB with DNA are both the insertion mode, GRCD will compete with the EB probe for the same insertion site, so the EB probe originally inserted into the DNA base pair to be freed, decreasing the fluorescence degree of the system. After adding GRCD, the fluorescence intensity of the DNA-EB system gradually decreased, suggesting the interaction mode between GRCD and DNA is an insertion (Fig. 3H).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Inhibition mechanism of GRCD on E. Coli
ce:section.ce:para: The effect of GRCD on DNA was further studied under visible light. Fig. 4 A shows the gel electrophoresis images of DNA in the GRCD-DNA system after 5 min of visible light irradiation (50 mW/cm2). DNA was significantly degraded under the action of GRCD and visible light. The reason may be that GRCD has oxidase-like properties, and can convert O2 into ROS under the catalytic oxidation of visible light and then oxidatively decompose DNA. Further experiments revealed that GRCD could gradually (0–60 s) oxidize TMB to blue under the irradiation of visible light (Fig. 4B), indicating that efficient ROS was produced by the GRCD solution system under the irradiation of visible light. To explore the type of ROS generated from GRCD catalysis, we conducted different ROS capture experiments and added different capture agents to the GRCD/TMB system, including tryptophan (1O2 scavenger), mannitol (•OH scavenger), catalase (H2O2 scavenger) and superoxide dismutase (O2• scavenger) (Fig. S4A–H). Only after tryptophan was added, the activity of GRCD oxidase was significantly reduced, indicating the GRCD photocatalytic oxidation process mainly produced 1O2. 2,2,6,6-Tetramethylpiperidine (TEMP) was used as the singlet oxygen (1O2) capture agent to measure the EPR spectrum of the GRCD solution (Fig. 4C). As a result, an obvious EPR spectrum signal was produced, which further proved the GRCD solution system produces 1O2 under visible light excitation.

The reason why GRCD can photocatalytically produce 1O2 is that GRCD has good properties of room temperature phosphorescence (RTP) photosensitizers [13,16,38]. The optimal wavelength of RTP excitation and emission for GRCD are 540 and 700 nm respectively (Fig. 4D), and the phosphorescence lifetime is 2.94 ms (Fig. 4E), and RTP is produced by triplet excited state (T1) transition. Therefore, RTP photosensitizers have very high T1-state exciton quantum yields. As ground-state O2 molecules are under a triplet state, higher-oxidizing 1O2 can be generated by RTP photosensitizers using energy transfer between O2 molecules and T1-state exciton(Fig. 4F) [39–41].

To further study 1O2 generating ability of GRCD solutions, 1,3-diphenylisobenzofuran (DPBF) was utilized as a trapping agent of 1O2, while a photosensitizer Phloxine B (PB) was used as the control (quantum yield = 0.65). Finally, 0.50 was calculated as the 1O2 quantum yield of GRCD (Fig. 4G–L), suggesting that GRCD has an efficient 1O2 quantum yield.

In summary, GRCD enters the bacterial body by destroying the LPS of the external membrane of E. coli, inserts and binds to the DNA of E. coli. At the same time, it generates 1O2 through photocatalysis under visible light to oxidatively decompose the DNA of E. coli into smaller fragments, destroying the DNA structure of E. coli and then playing an antibacterial role.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Practical applications of GRCD with antibacterial properties
ce:section.ce:para: The cytotoxicity of GRCD at different concentrations (0–30 μg/mL) to mouse fibroblasts NIH3T3 was measured using the CCK-8 method (Fig. S5A). The survival rate of the cell still reached 83.2 % when the GRCD concentration was 30 μg/mL, indicating GRCD has high biocompatibility at a certain concentration. In addition, GRCD can gradually self-decompose under visible light, and the GRCD decomposition rate can reach 94.5 % after 950 min of illumination (Fig. S5B), which avoids the potential harm of GRCD to the environment.

We also explored the application of GRCD in handling Gram-negative bacterial infections (E. coli) (Fig. 5 A). Four groups were set: (1) a PBS control group, (2) a PBS+Visible light group, (3) a GRCD+Dark group, and (4) a GRCD+Visible light group, and the wound area of each group were measured after 3, 7 or 14 days (Fig. 5B). According to the comparison with the PBS control group, GRCD+Dark and GRCD+Visible light groups showed better antibacterial effects, of which the GRCD+Visible light group had the best wound healing effect (Fig. 5C). For example, the wound healing rate of the GRCD+Visible light group on day 7 was 76.6 %, which was only 59.9 % in the PBS control group. Additionally, the newly formed tissues were examined via staining of hematoxylin and eosin (H&E) to better understand the anti-inflammatory effects of GRCD. After 14 days of treatment, the overall skin structure in the PBS control group was abnormal, the epidermal structure of the wound was missing, and the dermal layer of the skin was edematous, suggesting the structure was disordered. A large number of proliferated fibroblasts were arranged in a loose network structure, and network collagen fibers were produced. These changes were accompanied by certain infiltration of inflammatory cells. However, the overall structure of the skin in the GRCD+Visible light group was slightly abnormal. Clearly, the epidermal structure of the wound was intact and the wound began to keratinize, but no obvious abnormal keratinization occurred. The structure of the dermal layer was complete and the collagen fibers were abundant. The structure was dense with only slight inflammatory cell infiltration (Fig. 5D). Furthermore, we also studied the effects of GRCD on liver and kidney function indicators Alanine aminotransferase (ALT), albumin (ALB), urea (URA), total bilirubin (TBIL), creatinine (CREA), aspartate aminotransferase (AST) in the experimental mice. Compared with the control group, after 14 days, either each group remained unchanged, or the GRCD and the GRCD+Visible light groups were the best (Fig. S6A–F), indicating GRCD treatment and GRCD+Visible light groups treatment had no effect on the liver and kidney functions of mice or were beneficial to enhancing liver and kidney function indicators in mice. The above results showed that the GRCD+Visible light group achieved the best wound healing effect, and GRCD can be used as a visible light-enhanced therapeutic agent.

In summary, this study synthesized a Gd3+ polymer carbon dot (GRCD) using the approved therapeutic diagnostic agents (RB) and Gd3+, and used GRCD to restrain the growth of Gram-negative bacteria (E. coli as an example). Gd3+ in GRCD exhibits high dark-state antibacterial activity since it can bind to LPS in the external membranes of E. coli and destroy the structure of the external membranes of E. coli. Moreover, GRCD can bind to DNA in an inserted manner. 1O2 is produced under visible light irradiation (quantum yield 0.50), which can further enhance the antibacterial activity of GRCD through photocatalytic oxidation and decomposition of DNA without obvious cytotoxicity to NIH3T3. According to the results, a new idea has been provided to design and synthesize new carbon dot nanocomposite antibacterial agents against Gram-negative bacteria.

Yanming Miao: Conceptualization, Investigation, Data curation, Writing – original draft, Funding acquisition. Tao Zhang: Investigation, Data curation. Xujuan Zhao: Investigation. Xiaojie Sun: Investigation, Data curation. Jinzhi Lv: Conceptualization, Funding acquisition, Supervision.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The work was completed under the support of National Natural Science Foundation of China (NSFC) (Grant 32171393, 32001202 and 31700862).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.saa.2024.125158.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
The following are the Supplementary data to this article:
